1.synthesis: Eliphaz opens the third cycle with the most direct accusation in the book. The framing question discloses his thesis: “Can a man be profitable to God? Surely he who is wise is profitable to himself. Is it any pleasure to the Almighty if you are in the right, or is it gain to him if you make your ways blameless?” (Job 22:2–3). Since God gains nothing from human righteousness, Eliphaz argues, God’s reproof of Job cannot be for Job’s piety—it must be for Job’s wickedness: “Is not your evil abundant? Is there no end to your iniquities?” (Job 22:5). He then lists specific accusations: Job took pledges from brothers without cause, stripped the naked of their clothing (Job 22:6), withheld water from the weary and bread from the hungry (Job 22:7), sent widows away empty and crushed the arms of the fatherless (Job 22:9). Therefore snares surround Job, sudden terror overwhelms him (Job 22:10). God sees from the height of heaven (Job 22:12), and the generation of the wicked was swept away as a warning (Job 22:15–18). Eliphaz closes with the righteous rejoicing over the wicked’s fate (Job 22:19–20).
1.historical_context: Eliphaz’s catalogue of social sins (vv. 6–9)—pledges taken, naked stripped, bread withheld, widows dismissed, fatherless crushed—is drawn directly from the prophetic catalogue of covenantal violation that appears in Amos, Isaiah, and Micah (cf. Amos 2:6–7; Isa 1:17; Mic 2:2). These are the classic indictments of the Deuteronomic covenant lawsuit. The theological argument of vv. 2–3—that God is not benefited by human righteousness—is true as a statement about divine self-sufficiency but is used here as a logical step toward accusing Job: since God gains nothing from piety, the only reason God would reprove someone is for genuine wickedness. This syllogism, ironically, is false in Job’s case and is precisely the framework the prologue has already exposed as inadequate.
1.literary_structure: Chapter 22 opens the third cycle (chs. 22–27), which is marked by shorter friend-speeches and longer Job-speeches than the earlier cycles. Eliphaz’s third speech divides sharply: accusation (vv. 1–20) and call to repentance (vv. 21–30). The accusation section escalates far beyond anything the friends have said previously: they have implied Job was guilty through fate-of-the-wicked oracles; Eliphaz now lists Job’s specific crimes. The specificity is both the speech’s rhetorical power and its theological failure: these are invented charges applied to a man the narrator has described as blameless and upright (1:1), who defended the poor and the widow throughout his life (ch29).
1.theological_application: Eliphaz’s fabricated accusations (22:6–9) are the theological crisis of the third cycle: not simple misapplication of correct principles but the invention of specific crimes to fit a prior verdict. The movement from “you must be guilty of something” to specific accusations of robbing widows and crushing orphans is the deductive trap that retributionism creates—if suffering implies guilt, and the sins must match the suffering, the theologian begins fabricating evidence. Communities engaging with Job are warned that the most serious pastoral failure is not a wrong theory but the willingness to invent facts to protect the theory. God’s verdict in ch42 is not only that the friends’ theology was misapplied but that they “did not speak of me what is right” (42:7).
1.christology: Eliphaz’s accusation that Job withheld bread from the hungry, dismissed widows, and crushed the fatherless (22:7–9) is the exact inverse of what Job actually did (ch29–31) and of what Christ actually is. Christ himself is the bread for the hungry (John 6:35), the father of the fatherless (Ps 68:5), the one who welcomes the widow. The theological procedure Eliphaz uses—inferring guilt from suffering and then filling in crimes to match—was used against Christ himself: the crowd “sought false testimony against Jesus that they might put him to death” (Matt 26:59). Job and Christ both face fabricated indictments from those who assume that suffering proves guilt.

21.synthesis: Eliphaz’s speech pivots from accusation to restoration with unexpected tenderness: “Agree with God and be at peace; thereby good will come to you. Receive instruction from his mouth and lay up his words in your heart” (Job 22:21–22). “If you return to the Almighty you will be built up; if you remove injustice far from your tents, if you lay gold in the dust, and gold of Ophir among the stones of the torrent bed, then the Almighty will be your gold and your precious silver” (Job 22:23–25). “Then you will delight yourself in the Almighty and lift up your face to God” (Job 22:26). “You will pray to him and he will hear you” (Job 22:27). What Job decrees will be established; light will shine on his ways (Job 22:28). “For God abases the proud but saves the one who is lowly” (Job 22:29). Remarkably, Eliphaz closes with a hint at intercessory power: “He delivers even the one who is not innocent, who will be delivered through the cleanness of your hands” (Job 22:30). This is ironic prophecy: by the end of the book, Job will intercede for the friends themselves.
21.historical_context: The call to repentance in vv. 21–30 is the most beautiful passage in any of the friends’ speeches, drawing on the prophetic tradition of restoration-after-repentance (cf. Isa 55:6–7; Hos 6:1). The promise that the Almighty will be Job’s gold (22:25) if he lays aside earthly gold echoes the paradox of divine wealth that Proverbs also articulates (Prov 3:14). The closing verse about delivering “the one who is not innocent” through the clean hands of the repentant (22:30) anticipates the actual resolution: God will indeed tell the three friends to go to Job for intercession, and Job’s prayer will secure their deliverance (42:8). Eliphaz speaks better than he knows.
21.literary_structure: The call-to-repentance section (vv. 21–30) is the rhetorical climax of Eliphaz’s entire contribution to the dialogue. It is also the most painful section: it offers genuine spiritual goods (delight in the Almighty, heard prayer, light on one’s ways) to a man who already has them and who has been maintaining his integrity precisely while Eliphaz denies it. The irony is layered: the conditions for restoration Eliphaz describes—returning to God, removing injustice, laying gold aside—are conditions Job has never violated. He has nothing to return from. The call to repentance is both the most beautiful and the most wrong of Eliphaz’s speeches because it assumes an estrangement from God that Job’s complaint is itself evidence against.
21.theological_application: Eliphaz’s call to repentance contains genuine wisdom about the shape of spiritual restoration: agreeing with God, receiving instruction, treating gold as less than God, delight in the Almighty, heard prayer, light on one’s ways. These are true descriptions of the life of faith and of the fruit of genuine repentance. The problem is not the content of the call but its addressee: Job has not departed from these things. Communities shaped by Job learn that correct spiritual direction given to the wrong person in the wrong diagnosis is both useless and cruel. Eliphaz’s closing verse (22:30) about deliverance through clean hands is the accidental prophecy of the very resolution God will enact: Job’s clean hands will be the means of the friends’ own deliverance.
21.christology: Eliphaz’s vision of the restored man who prays and is heard, whose decrees are established, whose face is lifted to God (22:26–28), describes what is true of Christ and, in union with Christ, of those who are his. The delight in the Almighty as gold (22:25) is the treasure Paul describes: “I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord” (Phil 3:8). The closing verse about delivering the non-innocent through clean hands (22:30) is fulfilled first in Christ—the cleanness of whose hands delivers those who are not innocent—and then in Job’s actual intercession for the friends at the end of the book: “the prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working” (James 5:16).
